title: JSC370 Final Project
text:
Every time course selection rolls around, you can spot the furious searching from every student, browsing their rating website of choice to determine which courses, and by extension professors, to choose. The two most common? Rate My Professor and the official Course Evaluations.
In this project, we compare and contrast these two methods: one official and one personal. We will first do some exploratory analysis on each dataset, exploring how they compare in several key areas, followed by some statistical analysis & modeling.
Note: the contents of this website is primarily for supporting the main Project Report. There will be some analysis in the table captions, however the bulk of it will lie within the project report.


The data used in this analysis comes from two sources: U of T Evaluations and Rate My Professor. The U of T Course Evaluations dataset was collected via webscraping and contain a per-section instructor ratings across nine dimensions (INS1–INS6, ARTSC1-ARTSC-3) for courses spanning multiple years. The RateMyProfessor (RMP) dataset was scraped from the public RMP website and includes per-rating helpfulness, clarity, and difficulty scores, as well as free-text comments and tag selections left by students voluntarily.
Note that the tables shown below do not represent the full dataset, and should only be used for illustrative purposes. The the dataset descriptions and raw files are available at github.com/ChristopherLi05/UofT-Course-Data.




                            
                                            







                            
                                            






If you look at the original dataset, you may have noticed that the fields do not match. However, as professors are primarily rated based upon the courses that they teach, we can assume that the average of the INS categories are how students will actually rate their professor.




                            
                                            


Figure 1: Distribution of instructor ratings from official course evaluations and RateMyProfessor (RMP).




Official evaluations are heavily concentrated near 4.2–4.39, while RMP ratings are much flatter, suggesting that students who voluntarily rate professors on RMP tend to give more critical assessments than those captured in mandatory course evaluations.




                            
                                            


Figure 2: Scatter plot of each professor’s average official INS score vs their average RMP rating. Each point represents one professor matched across both datasets, with at least 5 ratings in each system.




A positive trend is present, indicating that the RMP Rating & INS Score tends to be fairly correlated. This is to be expected.
As a baseline comparison, a simple OLS regression was fit predicting average RMP rating from average INS score alone.

(Table 1)
The OLS model finds a significant positive relationship between INS score and RMP rating: for every 1-point increase in INS score, RMP rating is predicted to increase by approximately 1.56 points. However, the model only explains 34.8% of variance in RMP ratings, which may be an issue.




                            
                                            


Figure 3: Residual plot from OLS regression of RMP rating on official INS score. Each point is one professor.




The residual plot shows a clear funnel, which violates some OLS assumptions. A more complex model is required.




                            
                                            


Figure 4: Scatter plot of average difficulty vs average rating on RateMyProfessor, restricted to professors with at least 5 ratings.




A negative trend is present, suggesting students rate harder professors lower. This is to be expected.
As a baseline comparison, a simple OLS regression was fit predicting average RMP rating from average difficulty score alone.

(Table 2)
The OLS model finds a significant negative relationship between difficulty and RMP rating: for every 1-point increase in difficulty, RMP rating is predicted to decrease by approximately 0.90 points. Both the intercept and difficulty coefficient are highly significant (p < 0.001), and the model explains 32.7% of variance in RMP ratings.




                            
                                            


Figure 5: Residual plot from OLS regression of RMP rating on average difficulty, restricted to professors with at least 5 ratings.




The residual plot shows a clear funnel, which violates some OLS assumptions. A more complex model is required.




                            
                                            


Figure 6: Top 20 most frequently used rating tags left by students on RateMyProfessor, restricted to professors with at least 5 ratings.




Tags reflect common themes in student experience. There is a mix of both positive and negative tags.




                            
                                            


Figure 7: Distribution of VADER compound sentiment scores from RateMyProfessor comments, restricted to professors with at least 5 ratings. Scores range from -1 (most negative) to +1 (most positive).




We notice a slight bimodal distribution, with most comments concentrating around very positive. This is fairly interesting to see.





We previously explored that simple OLS’s are not suitable for modeling some of this data. Hence, we try GAMs to see how well they perform.
A multiple OLS regression was fit predicting average RMP rating from average INS score and average difficulty.

(Table 3)
Both predictors are highly significant (p < 0.001). Average INS score has a positive effect on RMP rating: a 1-point increase in INS score is associated with approximately a 1.17-point increase in RMP rating. Conversely, avgDifficulty has a negative effect: a 1-point increase in difficulty is associated with approximately a 0.67-point decrease in RMP rating. A test R² of 0.59 indicates that only about 59% of the data’s variance is explained by the model.




                            
                                            


Figure 8: Residual plot from OLS regression predicting RMP rating from average INS score and average difficulty. Each point represents one professor in the test set.




A clear funnel shape is present, indicating that more complex models are needed.
The GAM model was fit using two smooth terms: average INS score (s(0)) and average RMP difficulty (s(1)), predicting a professor’s average RMP rating. Smoothing parameters were selected via grid search cross-validation.

(Table 4)
Both smooth terms are highly significant (p < 0.001), indicating that both the official INS score and RMP difficulty contribute meaningfully to predicting RMP ratings. The effective degrees of freedom (EDoF) greater than 1 for both terms confirms that the relationships are nonlinear, justifying the use of a GAM over a simpler linear model. The model explains approximately 59% of variance on the test set (R² = 0.59). We achieve smilar R² compared to the OLS model, but the violation of assumptions imply that this is the correct model to use.




                            
                                            


Figure 9: GAM partial dependence plots predicting RMP average rating from official INS score and average difficulty, for professors with at least 5 ratings in each system. Shaded regions represent 95% confidence intervals. We fit a singular GAM with the two predictors we are interested in.




This follows a similar pattern as earler, where there is a positive correlation with average INS score, and a negative correlation with difficulty.



Next, we explore some common words used in the RMP ratings.




                            
                                            


Figure 10: LDA of the most common word per category








This project was set out to compare two different methods of rating U of T university professors: the official course evaluations and the RateMyProfessors platform. Through our statistical analysis, these are our findings.
First, the two rating systems differ in terms of their distributions. Official evaluations cluster around 4.2–4.39, reflecting the tendency of the institutional surveys to produce inflated and compressed scores. RMP ratings, on the other hand, are fairly spread out. This doesn’t seem to follow a bimodal distribution as what self-report bias may include, but this may be due to the aggregation method used when plotting the data.
Despite the distribution differences, these two systems are fairly correlated. The scatter plot and OLS regression confirm a significant positive relationship between a professor’s average INS score and their average RMP rating, suggesting that these are both measuring the same underlying teaching quality.
However, the residual plots suggest that several OLS assumptions are violated, so we use GAM’s to model this relationship instead. We controlled for course difficulty as a second predictor, as from anecdotal experience, it is one area which affects a student’s rating of the course. The GAM confirmed the nonlinear relationships for both predictors while achieving comparable predictive performance.
Finally, LDA topic modeling of RMP comments revealed five interpretable themes in student language: workload and assignments, positive reviews, teaching quality, personality, and negative experiences. This suggests that comments capture a different portion of the student experience that cannot be explained through numbers alone.
Together, the results show that these systems are fairly complementary. Students would benefit from considering both sources when deciding on which courses and professors to take.

title: JSC370 Final Project
text:
This project was set out to compare two different methods of rating U of T university professors: the official course evaluations and the RateMyProfessors platform. Through our statistical analysis, these are our findings.
First, the two rating systems differ in terms of their distributions. Official evaluations cluster around 4.2–4.39, reflecting the tendency of the institutional surveys to produce inflated and compressed scores. RMP ratings, on the other hand, are fairly spread out. This doesn’t seem to follow a bimodal distribution as what self-report bias may include, but this may be due to the aggregation method used when plotting the data.
Despite the distribution differences, these two systems are fairly correlated. The scatter plot and OLS regression confirm a significant positive relationship between a professor’s average INS score and their average RMP rating, suggesting that these are both measuring the same underlying teaching quality.
However, the residual plots suggest that several OLS assumptions are violated, so we use GAM’s to model this relationship instead. We controlled for course difficulty as a second predictor, as from anecdotal experience, it is one area which affects a student’s rating of the course. The GAM confirmed the nonlinear relationships for both predictors while achieving comparable predictive performance.
Finally, LDA topic modeling of RMP comments revealed five interpretable themes in student language: workload and assignments, positive reviews, teaching quality, personality, and negative experiences. This suggests that comments capture a different portion of the student experience that cannot be explained through numbers alone.
Together, the results show that these systems are fairly complementary. Students would benefit from considering both sources when deciding on which courses and professors to take.

title: JSC370 Code Exploration
text:
Data Exploration
This is a collection of code chunks that were used for data exploration. This is archived for prosterity’s sake.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.api as sm
import numpy as np
import json
import plotly.express as px

import nltk

nltk.download("vader_lexicon")
from nltk.sentiment import SentimentIntensityAnalyzer


course_eval_df = pd.read_csv("../UofT-Course-Data/data/course_evals.csv")
rmf_ratings_df = pd.read_csv("../UofT-Course-Data/data/ratings.csv")
rmf_df = pd.read_csv("../UofT-Course-Data/data/ratemyprof.csv")

with open("../UofT-Course-Data/data/prof_mapping.json") as f:
    prof_mapping = json.load(f)

course_eval_df["avg_INS"] = course_eval_df[
    ["INS1","INS2","INS3","INS4","INS5","INS6"]
].mean(axis=1)

course_eval_df["avg_ARTSC"] = course_eval_df[
    ["ARTSC1","ARTSC2","ARTSC3"]
].mean(axis=1)


print("Course Evals:")
print(course_eval_df.describe())
print("RMF Ratings:")
print(rmf_ratings_df.describe())
print("RMF")
print(rmf_df.describe())

plt.figure()
sns.histplot(rmf_df[rmf_df["numRatings"] >= 5]["avgRating"], bins=25, kde=True)
plt.title("Distribution of Average Ratings (Professors with ≥5 Ratings)")
plt.xlabel("Average Rating")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.show()









plt.figure()
sns.scatterplot(
    data=course_eval_df,
    x="avg_INS",
    y="avg_ARTSC",
    alpha=0.3
)

plt.title("Average Instructor Score vs Average ARTSC Score")
plt.xlabel("Average INS Score")
plt.ylabel("Average ARTSC Score")

plt.show()









predictors = ["INS1","INS2","INS3","INS4","INS5","INS6",
              "ARTSC1","ARTSC2","ARTSC3"]

X = course_eval_df[predictors]
X = sm.add_constant(X)

successes = course_eval_df["number_responses"]
trials = course_eval_df["number_invited"]
y = pd.concat([successes, trials - successes], axis=1)

mask = X.notna().all(axis=1) & y.notna().all(axis=1)
X_clean = X[mask]
y_clean = y[mask]


glm_binom = sm.GLM(y_clean, X_clean, family=sm.families.Binomial())
results = glm_binom.fit()

print(results.summary())

odds_ratios = np.exp(results.params)
print("Odds ratios:\n", odds_ratios)
